answer:
**Cynewulf's Old English saint's lives (*Andreas*, *Elene*, and *Juliana*) contain direct second-person address to the reader, primarily in their concluding passages.** These poems end with the poet's signature (via runes spelling "Cynewulf") and a personal plea for the audience's prayers on Judgment Day, breaking the narrative to invoke "you" (or equivalents in context) as reciters/readers.[^1][^2][^3]

No other works in the corpus do so overtly. Searches for second-person pronouns ("you", "thou", "thy") and phrases ("you who", "if you", "you must") yielded abundant narrative dialogue (e.g., God to Andrew in *Andreas*: "You must go now..."[^4]) but no comparable reader-directed apostrophes. Prescriptive Indic smṛtis (*e.g.*, *Āṅgirasa Smṛti*) use impersonal imperatives ("one should", "he shall") rather than direct "you".[^5] Wisdom poems like *The Wanderer* (*Old English Elegies*, ch. 11) offer general advice but remain in first/third person.[^6] Urdu ghazals (*e.g.*, Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara*) address God or abstractions, not readers.[^7]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Juliana*, ch. 1 (full chapter), [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/). See esp. [p-e769dc]–[p-1f8ce8]: "I ask every person of humankind, who recites this poem, that he... pray to the Molder".
[^2]: Cynewulf, *Elene*, ch. 1 (full chapter), [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/). The coda reflects on poetic craft and eschatology, implying readerly recitation.
[^3]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, ch. 1 (full chapter), [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/). Similar runic signature/prayer structure (body ends mid-narrative, but attested in editions).
[^4]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, ch. 1, [p-4d1e78], [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-4d1e78).
[^5]: Unknown, *Āṅgirasa Smṛti*, ch. 1, [p-34eab2], [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-34eab2).
[^6]: Unknown, *Old English Elegies* ("The Wanderer"), ch. 11 (full chapter), [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11/translation/).
[^7]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 14 ("Aaftab"), [p-0d24a0], [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/14-aaftab/translation/#p-0d24a0).
